schools.summary: Being justified by faith (established in chs. 3-4), we have peace with God through Christ — the first fruit of justification; the manuscripts divide between echomen ('we have peace,' indicative) and echōmen ('let us have peace,' subjunctive), most editors preferring the subjunctive on textual grounds but the indicative suiting the doctrinal context (Robertson).

Henry: peace is more than the cessation of enmity — friendship and loving-kindness; the guilt that bred the quarrel removed, the sinner now at peace and a friend of God through Christ the peacemaker, the Mediator.

debates.question: Is the text 'we have peace' (indicative) or 'let us have peace' (subjunctive)?

sides.position: 'Let us have peace' (echōmen) — preferred on manuscript grounds by most editors
'We have peace' (echomen) — suits the doctrinal context, stating a fact of justification

themes: being justified by faith, we have peace with God
through our Lord Jesus Christ
the first fruit of justification
peace and reconciliation with God

schools.summary: Through Christ we have access (prosagoge, introduction, admission) by faith into this grace (favour) wherein we stand — a settled, secure position of favour (Barnes, Robertson) — and rejoice in hope of the glory of God, the joyful prospect of sharing God's glory.
Calvin (with Theodoret): justification brings not only present peace and standing but the joyful hope of future glory — the believer rejoicing in the prospect of sharing God's glory.

themes: access by faith into this grace wherein we stand
rejoice in hope of the glory of God
access, standing, and hope
the fruits of justification

schools.summary: The believer glories (rejoices, exults) not only in hope but even in tribulations (Barnes, Robertson) — finding joy in suffering itself — knowing that tribulation works patience (hupomone, endurance), affliction sanctified developing Christian character.
Calvin (with Theodoret): tribulation, far from destroying faith, strengthens it, working patience; the believer glories because affliction yields spiritual fruit.

themes: we glory in tribulations also
tribulation worketh patience
glorying in afflictions
suffering producing endurance

schools.summary: The chain continues — patience (endurance) works experience (dokime, proof, tried and approved character, as metal proved by fire, Barnes, Robertson), and experience works hope (the tested character strengthening the believer's hope of glory).
Calvin: the ascending chain — tribulation, patience, proof, hope; each step the fruit of grace, affliction ultimately strengthening the hope that justification gives.

themes: and patience, experience; and experience, hope
the chain of grace's work
tried character yielding hope
affliction deepening hope

schools.summary: This hope does not disappoint or put to shame (it will not fail, unlike worldly hopes, Barnes, Robertson), because the love of God (his love to us, most hold) is shed abroad (poured out, lavishly diffused) in our hearts by the Holy Ghost given to us — the Spirit's inward witness confirming the hope.
Cyril (with Calvin): the Holy Spirit, given to believers, sheds abroad the sense of God's love — the Spirit's witness the ground of assured hope.

themes: hope maketh not ashamed
the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost
the hope that does not disappoint
God's love poured out by the Spirit

schools.summary: When we were yet without strength (asthenes, helpless, powerless to save ourselves, Barnes, Robertson), in due time (the appointed moment in God's plan) Christ died for the ungodly (the impious) — the supreme demonstration of love, dying for those with no claim, strength, or merit.
Henry (with Calvin): the wonder — Christ died not for the righteous but for the ungodly, the supreme demonstration of love for the undeserving and helpless.

themes: when we were yet without strength
in due time Christ died for the ungodly
the supreme proof of God's love
Christ died for the helpless and undeserving

schools.summary: Among men, hardly anyone would die even for a righteous (just) man (such self-sacrifice rare); perhaps for a good man (benevolent, lovable) someone might dare to die — a higher love, yet still rare and only for the deserving (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the contrast magnifies God's love (v.8) — even the rare heights of human love are only for the good or righteous, but Christ died for sinners.

themes: scarcely for a righteous man will one die
for a good man some would even dare to die
the contrast with human love
human self-sacrifice only for the deserving

schools.summary: God commends (sunistesin, proves, demonstrates, exhibits) his love toward us in that, while we were yet sinners (his enemies, not waiting for us to become worthy), Christ died for us — love beyond all human love, which reaches only the good (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the surpassing greatness — love beyond compare, dying for sinners not the righteous; the proof and ground of all the believer's assurance.

themes: God commendeth his love toward us
while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us
the climactic proof of God's love
love for the unworthy

schools.summary: Paul reasons a fortiori (from greater to lesser): if God justified us by Christ's blood while sinners (the harder thing), much more will he save us from wrath (the easier); being now justified by his blood (the atoning death), we shall be saved from the final wrath through him — the future as sure as the present (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: being now justified by his blood
we shall be saved from wrath through him
the certainty of final salvation
the argument from greater to lesser

schools.summary: If, while we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by Christ's death (the harder, costlier work, for the hostile), much more, being now reconciled (his friends), shall we be saved by his life — Christ's risen, living intercession securing our final salvation (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Theodoret): the death reconciled, the life saves; if the costlier work was done for enemies, the easier is sure for friends — the two-fold ground of assurance in the dying and living Christ.

themes: when we were enemies, we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son
much more, being reconciled, we shall be saved by his life
the certainty from reconciliation
reconciled by his death, saved by his life

schools.summary: The believer not only is justified, has peace, and is assured of salvation, but joys (rejoices, exults) in God himself — the highest blessing — through our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom we have now received the atonement (katallage, reconciliation, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): 'atonement' means reconciliation — we have now received reconciliation through Christ, the ground of our joy in God.

themes: we also joy in God through our Lord Jesus Christ
by whom we have now received the atonement
the crowning fruit of joy in God
reconciliation through Christ

schools.summary: Through Adam's one sin, sin entered the world and death by sin, death passing on all; Barnes and Robertson read 'eph ho' as 'because all sinned' — death passed on all because all in fact sin personally (Barnes denying imputation of Adam's sin; Robertson noting the constative aorist gathering the race's history of sin).

Calvin (with Augustine, the Vulgate, Beza): 'in whom all sinned' — all sinned in Adam their representative and natural head, inheriting his guilt and the corruption of nature (original sin); we are imbued with natural depravity from the womb, which is sin before God and deserves his vengeance, refuting the Pelagian gloss of mere imitation.

Chrysostom and Theodoret: Adam, having fallen, became mortal and so begot a mortal race; all, as sprung from him, inherited a mortal nature; the parallel with Christ, who gains life to all, is set up.

debates.question: Does 'for that all have sinned' (eph ho) mean 'in whom [Adam] all sinned' or 'because all sinned'?

sides.position: 'In whom all sinned' — all sinned in Adam their head, inheriting his guilt and corruption (original sin)
'Because all sinned' — death passed on all because all in fact sin personally

themes: by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin
death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned
the Adam-Christ parallel
the original-sin crux

schools.summary: From Adam to Moses (before the law was given) sin was in the world — men sinned and died (Barnes, Robertson); though sin is not formally imputed where there is no explicit law, men nevertheless died, proving sin and its penalty were present without the written law.
Calvin (with Theodoret): the reign of death before the law proves sin (Adam's and men's own) was in the world; the law made sin more known but did not introduce it.

themes: until the law sin was in the world
sin is not imputed when there is no law
sin reigned before the law
the universality of sin and death from Adam

schools.summary: Death reigned (universal sway) from Adam to Moses, even over those who had not sinned exactly as Adam did (against a positive command) — including infants — proving their share in Adam's sin; and Adam is the figure (tupos, type) of Christ, the one whose act affected the many (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): Adam the type of him to come — the head whose act determined the many, paralleling Christ; death's reign over infants pointing to their participation in Adam's sin.

themes: death reigned from Adam to Moses
over them that had not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression
Adam the figure of him that was to come
death's universal reign and the type of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the offence' Adam's first sin (by which he fell and all his posterity with him), by 'the free gift' the righteousness of Christ which justifies, both Adam and Christ 'one man' for the comparison; the free gift not merely like the offence but far exceeding it — if by one man's offence many died, much more has the grace of God and the gift by Christ abounded (superabounded) unto many (the grace exceeding the ruin, the antitype surpassing the type, the second Adam greater than the first, the grace abounding over the sin, v.20), the two heads (Adam and Christ), the comfort of the superabounding grace (more than repairing the ruin).

themes: not as the offence, so also is the free gift
much more the grace of God... hath abounded unto many
grace abounding much more
the antitype surpassing the type

schools.summary: Barnes: the second point of difference — the evil consequences flowed from one crime, one act of guilt (Adam's), but the favours had respect to many acts of guilt (the gift covering many offences); the judgment came by one offence unto condemnation, but the free gift covers many offences unto justification — the grace not only exceeding but reaching further (justifying from the whole multitude of sins, Adam's one imputed sin and all the believer's actual sins, the breadth of the pardon), the comprehensive pardon (the believer acquitted of all his sins, the comfort of the full justification).

themes: the judgment was by one to condemnation
the free gift is of many offences unto justification
the gift covering many offences
the breadth of the pardon

schools.summary: Gill: 'by one offence death reigned by one,' for it was the single sin of Adam that gave death its reigning power over men; Barnes: under the administration of a just and merciful God, by the offence of one, death has reigned; if by one man's offence death reigned, much more shall those who receive the abundance of grace and the gift of righteousness reign in life by Christ — the believers not merely delivered from death's reign but made to reign in life (the fuller triumph than the ruin, the believers made kings, Revelation 22:5, the more-than-restoration, the grace abounding over the fall), the abundant grace and the imputed righteousness received by faith, the reign through the one Saviour.

themes: by one man's offence death reigned by one
shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ
believers reign in life
a fuller triumph than the ruin

schools.summary: Barnes: the summing up, resuming the proposition of v.12 — as by one offence judgment came on all to condemnation, so by one righteousness the free gift came on all unto justification of life; the parallel of the two federal heads (Adam's offence bringing condemnation on all his, Christ's righteousness bringing justification of life on all his); the two 'all' defined by their head — all in Adam (the whole race by nature) condemned, all in Christ (the believing) justified (not universalism, but all who are in Christ as all who are in Adam); the 'justification of life' (the acquittal issuing in life).

debates.question: Does 'the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life' teach that all are justified?

sides.position: No — the 'all' is defined by the head: all in Christ (the believing) are justified, parallel to all in Adam (the race) condemned; the gift received only by those in Christ
It expresses the universal sufficiency of the provision (the gift offered to all), though received only by those in Christ

themes: by the offence of one judgment came upon all men to condemnation
by the righteousness of one... justification of life
the two federal heads
the two 'all' defined by the head

schools.summary: Barnes (citing Calvin): not a mere repetition but an explanation — guarding against the inference that men were condemned without guilt (the apostle affirms they are in fact sinners; lest any arrogate to himself innocence, Paul shows men were made sinners by Adam); by Adam's one disobedience many were made (constituted, reckoned) sinners, and by Christ's one obedience many shall be made righteous — the parallel imputation (Adam's disobedience imputed constituting the race sinners, Christ's obedience, active and passive, imputed constituting the believing righteous), 'made' = constituted by imputation, not made inherently by infusion (the forensic constituting).

debates.question: Does 'made sinners/made righteous' mean inherently made or constituted by imputation?

sides.position: Constituted/reckoned by imputation (forensic) — not made inherently by infusion; the disobedience and obedience imputed

themes: by one man's disobedience many were made sinners
by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous
the parallel imputation
the heart of imputation

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the law' chiefly the moral law as it came by Moses (entering with great solemnity on Sinai, coming in between Adam's sin and Christ's sacrifice, over and above the promise), by which sin abounded and appeared very sinful; the law entered that the offence might abound — revealing and increasing the knowledge and guilt of sin (making it appear exceeding sinful, Romans 7:13, not causing but revealing and aggravating it, the law's convicting work); but where sin abounded, grace did much more abound (superabound, exceeding the abounding sin, the triumph of grace over sin), the comfort guarded (no license to sin, Romans 6:1, grace abounding to deliver).

themes: the law entered, that the offence might abound
where sin abounded, grace did much more abound
the law making sin appear sinful
the triumph of superabounding grace

schools.summary: Gill: this illustrates the grace of God by comparing the reigns of sin and grace — sin has a kingly, governing, commanding power, its dominion universal over men in nature, but grace reigns to a contrary end; that as sin reigned unto death, even so grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Christ — the two reigns contrasted (the tyranny of sin ending in death, the reign of grace unto eternal life), grace reigning 'through righteousness' (not apart from it but through the imputed righteousness of Christ, the throne of grace grounded in the righteousness), unto eternal life (Romans 6:23), crowning the great argument (Romans 3-5).

themes: as sin hath reigned unto death
grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life
the two reigns contrasted
the triumph of reigning grace
